section.title: The Woman Caught in Adultery
section.sectionRationale: This brief, famous scene follows the division at Tabernacles. The scribes and Pharisees drag a woman caught in adultery before Jesus, using her as bait in a trap: if He upholds stoning He seems merciless, if He forbids it He seems to defy Moses. Jesus disarms the trap with a single sentence, and one by one the accusers leave, until only the guilty woman and the sinless Judge remain. He neither excuses her sin nor condemns her, but sends her out forgiven and changed.
section.thesis: Confronted with a trap baited by a guilty woman, Jesus exposes the accusers' own sin so that they melt away, and then, as the only one with the right to condemn, He extends mercy without excusing sin, sending her away neither condemned nor unchanged but forgiven and called to a new life.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the trap laid by the scribes and Pharisees; Jesus' sentence that turns the judgment on the accusers, who leave one by one; and the meeting of the sinless Judge and the guilty woman, ending in mercy and the call to sin no more.

units.label: The trap
units.note: a woman used to test Jesus

verses.text: Then each went to his own home.
But Jesus went to the Mount of Olives.
Early in the morning He went back into the temple courts. All the people came to Him, and He sat down to teach them.
The scribes and Pharisees, however, brought to Him a woman caught in adultery. They made her stand before them
and said, “Teacher, this woman was caught in the act of adultery.
In the Law Moses commanded us to stone such a woman. So what do You say?”
They said this to test Him, in order to have a basis for accusing Him. But Jesus bent down and began to write on the ground with His finger.

units.label: Let him who is without sin
units.note: the accusers leave one by one

verses.text: When they continued to question Him, He straightened up and said to them, “Let him who is {{anamartetos|without sin}} among you be the first to cast a stone at her.”
And again He bent down and wrote on the ground.
When they heard this, they began to go away one by one, beginning with the older ones, until only Jesus was left, with the woman standing there.

units.label: Neither do I condemn you
units.note: mercy and the call to a new life

verses.text: Then Jesus straightened up and asked her, “Woman, where are your accusers? Has no one condemned you?”
“No one, Lord,” she answered. “Then neither do I {{katakrino|condemn}} you,” Jesus declared. “Now go and {{hamartano|sin no more}}.”

anamartetos.gloss: Without sin, sinless. Jesus' single condition, let the one who is without sin throw the first stone, turns the searchlight on the accusers. The word does not deny that adultery is sin or that justice matters; it exposes that the accusers are themselves sinners, unfit to execute a self-righteous judgment. Only one truly sinless person stood there, and He chose mercy.

katakrino.gloss: To condemn, pronounce guilty and sentence. Jesus, the only one present with the right to condemn, does not. His words neither deny her guilt nor sentence her; He releases her from condemnation. This is the same word Paul uses in Romans 8:1, there is now no condemnation for those in Christ Jesus, the mercy this scene enacts in miniature.

hamartano.gloss: To sin, to miss the mark, to do wrong. Jesus' final words, go and sin no more, show that His mercy is not indifference to sin. Grace forgives the past and calls to a changed future; the woman is not merely let off but sent out to a new life. Mercy and the call to holiness belong together in Jesus.

basic.context: As the crowds gather in the temple at dawn, the scribes and Pharisees interrupt Jesus' teaching by dragging in a woman caught in adultery. Their question is a trap: the Law commanded stoning, so if Jesus agrees He seems harsh and may clash with Rome, but if He refuses He seems to set aside Moses. Jesus stoops and writes on the ground, then says only, let the one without sin throw the first stone. Convicted, the accusers leave one by one. Alone with the woman, Jesus says He does not condemn her, and tells her to go and sin no more.
basic.hermeneutics: The key questions are how Jesus can be both just and merciful, and what forgiveness does with sin. He does not deny that adultery is wrong or that the Law is real; He exposes the hypocrisy of self-righteous accusers and then, as the only sinless one, extends mercy that does not excuse but transforms. Note also the honest textual question: this passage is absent from the earliest manuscripts, yet the church has long received it as a true account consistent with the character of Jesus.
basic.originalLanguages: The scene turns on three words. Jesus asks for the one who is anamartetos, without sin, to cast the first stone, exposing the accusers. He then refuses to katakrino, to condemn, the guilty woman. And He sends her out with the command not to hamartano, not to sin again, so that mercy and the call to holiness go together.
basic.typology: Jesus writing with His finger on the ground recalls the finger of God that wrote the Law on stone; here the Lawgiver Himself brings mercy that the Law could not give. The scene anticipates the greater judgment scene at the cross, where the sinless one bears the condemnation sinners deserve.
basic.christ: Christ is the only sinless one present, the one truly qualified to condemn, who instead forgives. He upholds the truth about sin while extending grace, showing the very heart of His mission: not to condemn the world but to save it.
basic.god: God is revealed as both holy and merciful. He does not pretend sin away, yet He delights to forgive the guilty who have no defense. The scene displays the God who justifies the ungodly without cheapening righteousness.
basic.crossReferences: John 3:17 says God sent the Son to save, not condemn. Romans 8:1 declares no condemnation in Christ. Romans 2 exposes those who judge others while doing the same. Micah 7 rejoices in the God who pardons sin.
basic.oneStory: The sinless Judge meets a guilty sinner and, instead of the stones her accusers demanded, gives mercy that sends her out changed. It is a small window onto the whole gospel: the one without sin takes the place of judgment so that the guilty may go free and be made new.

context.literary: A self-contained scene of confrontation and reversal, set in the temple at dawn. It follows the pattern of the leaders' attempts to trap Jesus and turns their trap back on them. The narrative is spare and dramatic, framed by Jesus stooping to write, and it climaxes not in a legal ruling but in a personal word of mercy. It sits between the division of chapter 7 and the light of the world discourse that follows.
context.historical: The Law of Moses prescribed death for adultery, Leviticus 20:10 and Deuteronomy 22:22, and required witnesses; notably the man is absent here, exposing the accusers' bad faith. Under Roman rule the Jewish authorities generally could not carry out executions, sharpening the trap. Jesus' writing on the ground has been much discussed; the text does not say what He wrote, and its power lies in the sentence He speaks.
context.canonical: This passage, the pericope adulterae, is absent from the earliest and best Greek manuscripts of John and appears in varied locations in others, so most scholars judge it was not part of John's original text. Yet it has been received in the church for centuries as an authentic account of Jesus, deeply consistent with His character as shown throughout the Gospels. It vividly enacts the truth of John 3:17 and Romans 8:1, that Christ came to save rather than condemn.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To display Jesus turning a malicious trap into an occasion of mercy, upholding the reality of sin while refusing to condemn the guilty, and to reveal in one scene the heart of His mission: grace that forgives and calls to a transformed life.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The scene describes a particular encounter, not a rule that abolishes justice or church discipline. Its abiding truths are that self-righteous condemnation is exposed before the sinless Christ, that Jesus forgives the guilty who come to Him, and that His forgiveness always calls to a changed life.

debates.question: Is this passage part of the original text of John?

views.label: A later addition, not original to John
views.case: The earliest and best manuscripts omit it, its style differs somewhat from John, and it floats to different places in the manuscript tradition, so the strongest textual evidence indicates it was not written by John here.

views.label: An authentic tradition, rightly received
views.case: Its wide acceptance in the church, its ring of truth, and its perfect consistency with the character of Jesus have led many to treasure it as a genuine account of something Jesus did, even if not part of John's original text.

debates.bearing: Honesty requires acknowledging the textual question; either way, no doctrine rests on this passage alone, and its portrait of Christ's mercy is confirmed everywhere else in the Gospels, so it may be read with humility and thankfulness.

debates.question: Does Jesus' mercy set aside the seriousness of sin?

views.label: No, He upholds sin's gravity
views.case: Jesus never calls the adultery anything but sin, and He commands her to sin no more; His mercy addresses a real transgression rather than pretending none occurred.

views.label: His grace transforms, not merely excuses
views.case: The forgiveness is not a loophole but a release that summons her to a new life; grace and the call to holiness are held together, not opposed.

debates.bearing: Both guard against cheap grace: Jesus forgives real sin fully and freely, and that very forgiveness calls the forgiven to go and sin no more.

study.originalLanguages: The drama hinges on three words. Jesus makes one condition for the stoning: let the one who is anamartetos, without sin, cast the first stone, and the searchlight swings from the woman to her accusers, who quietly withdraw. Left alone with her, He refuses to katakrino, to condemn, though as the only sinless one present He alone had the right to. And He sends her away with a command, go and hamartano no more, do not sin again, so that His mercy is neither indulgent nor cold: it forgives the past fully and calls to a holy future.

typology.type: The finger of God writing the Law on stone, Exodus 31:18
typology.fulfillment: The Lawgiver Himself stooping to write on the ground, bringing the mercy the Law could not give, John 8:6 and 8.

typology.type: The demand of the Law for the sinner's death
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the sinless one, who bears condemnation Himself at the cross so the guilty may go free, Romans 8:1-3.
The final silencing of every accuser of God's people through the forgiveness Christ secures, Romans 8:33-34.

study.christ: Christ stands as the only sinless person in the scene, the one truly qualified to pick up a stone, and He is the one who refuses. He does not deny the woman's guilt or soften the Law's verdict on sin; He exposes the hypocrisy of her accusers and then extends the mercy He came to bring. In refusing to condemn, He reveals the purpose of His whole mission, for God sent the Son not to condemn the world but to save it. And in commanding her to sin no more, He shows that His grace makes new. The scene foreshadows the cross, where the sinless one takes the condemnation of sinners so they can walk free.
study.god: God is shown to be at once holy and merciful, never one at the expense of the other. He takes sin seriously, so Jesus calls it sin and calls her from it; yet He delights to pardon the guilty who stand defenseless before Him. This is the God of Micah 7, who pardons iniquity and does not retain His anger forever, and the God of Romans, who justifies the ungodly. In Jesus, the judgment sinners deserve gives way to mercy that heals, and the God who could rightly condemn instead sets the captive free and calls her to life.

crossReferences.note: God did not send His Son to condemn the world, but to save it, the very posture Jesus takes here.
There is now no condemnation for those in Christ Jesus, the mercy this scene enacts.
You who judge others do the same things, exposing the self-righteous accusers.
Who is a God like You, pardoning iniquity, the character of God displayed in Jesus' mercy.

study.oneStory: In one brief scene the whole gospel is pictured. The sinless Judge meets a guilty sinner with no defense, and instead of the stones her accusers demanded, He gives mercy that sends her out forgiven and changed. He upholds the truth about sin and yet does not condemn, because He came to save. Here in miniature is what the cross will accomplish: the one without sin standing in the place of judgment so that the guilty may go free, released from condemnation and called to a new life. The accusers fall silent, and grace has the last word.

quiz.q: What was the aim of the scribes and Pharisees in bringing the woman to Jesus?

options.text: To trap Him, so they would have a basis for accusing Him.
options.note: Verse 6 states plainly they said this to test Him and find grounds to accuse Him.

options.text: To seek His honest counsel on a hard case.
options.note: Their motive was a trap, not a sincere request for wisdom.

options.text: To force Him to choose between mercy and the Law of Moses.
options.note: True, this was the shape of the trap, but the text's stated aim is to have a basis for accusing Him.

options.text: To have the woman publicly shamed.
options.note: She was used as bait, but the real target of the scheme was Jesus, not merely her shame.

quiz.q: How does Jesus respond to the demand that the woman be stoned?

options.text: He says the one without sin should cast the first stone, exposing the accusers.
options.note: Verse 7 records the sentence that turns the judgment back on the accusers.

options.text: He declares that the Law of Moses no longer applies.
options.note: He does not abolish the Law; He exposes the hypocrisy of the accusers.

options.text: He simply refuses to answer their question.
options.note: He does answer, with a piercing sentence, rather than merely refusing.

options.text: He challenges the accusers to examine their own hearts first.
options.note: True in effect, but it softens the specific and disarming condition He set, the one without sin throwing first.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say to the woman when they are left alone?

options.text: Neither do I condemn you; now go and sin no more.
options.note: Verse 11 joins mercy and the call to a changed life.

options.text: Your sin does not matter; go in peace.
options.note: He never says her sin does not matter; He tells her to sin no more.

options.text: I forgive you, so you are free to live as you please.
options.note: His forgiveness is real, but it comes with the command to sin no more, not license.

options.text: I do not condemn you, and He lets her go.
options.note: True but incomplete; it omits the vital command, go and sin no more.

quiz.q: What does this scene reveal about how Jesus holds justice and mercy together?

options.text: He neither denies her sin nor condemns her, forgiving fully and calling her to a new life.
options.note: He upholds the reality of sin and yet extends mercy, the heart of the scene.

options.text: He shows that mercy means ignoring sin altogether.
options.note: He does not ignore sin; He names it and calls her from it while forgiving her.

options.text: He proves that justice must always give way to leniency.
options.note: It is not mere leniency; the sinless Judge extends costly mercy without excusing sin.

options.text: He forgives her and directs her toward a changed life.
options.note: True, but it understates that He also upholds the seriousness of her sin even as He forgives.

quiz.q: What is the honest textual situation of this passage?

options.text: It is absent from the earliest manuscripts, yet long received by the church as a true account consistent with Jesus' character.
options.note: This reflects the manuscript evidence and the church's reception, held with humility.

options.text: It is found in every manuscript with no question at all.
options.note: The earliest and best manuscripts omit it; this is a genuine textual question.

options.text: It is a proven forgery with no value.
options.note: That overstates the case; while likely not original to John, it has been treasured as consistent with Christ's character.

options.text: It is missing from some early manuscripts but widely accepted in the tradition.
options.note: True and close, but it understates how strong the early evidence for omission is.

questions: The accusers were quick to judge until Jesus turned the light on their own hearts. Where are you tempted to condemn others while overlooking your own sin?
Jesus, the only one qualified to condemn, chose mercy. How does it move you to know that the sinless Judge does not condemn those who come to Him?
Forgiveness came with the call, go and sin no more. What would it look like to receive both the mercy and the summons to a changed life?
The woman had no defense, yet she was not turned away. How does this steady you when you come to God aware of your own guilt?
The scene pictures grace that costs Jesus everything at the cross. How does the price of your forgiveness shape the way you live in response?

translationNotes.note: This passage, the woman caught in adultery, is not found in the earliest and best Greek manuscripts of John and appears at differing places in others, so most scholars judge it was not part of John's original text. The BSB prints it with a footnote to that effect. The church has long received it as an authentic account of Jesus, and it is included here as printed in the BSB, with the manuscript question noted honestly.
The text does not say what Jesus wrote on the ground; interpretations abound, but the power of the scene lies in the sentence He speaks, not in the writing.
The Law prescribed death for adultery (Leviticus 20:10; Deuteronomy 22:22) and required witnesses; the conspicuous absence of the man exposes the accusers' bad faith.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 7:53-8:11. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped, embedded speaker turns joined with single spaces. The passage is a known textual variant, absent from the earliest manuscripts, and is included as printed in the BSB with the manuscript question noted in translationNotes. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
